2605.06294.title: Log-Likelihood, Simpson's Paradox, and the Detection of Machine-Generated Text
2605.06294.paper_summary: The ability to reliably distinguish human-written text from that generated by large language models is of profound societal importance. The dominant approach to this problem exploits the likelihood hypothesis: that machine-generated text should appear more probable to a detector language model than human-written text. However, we demonstrate that the token-level signal distinguishing human and machine text is non-uniform across the hidden space of the detector model, and naively averaging likelihood-based token scores across regions with fundamentally different statistical structure, as most detectors do, causes a form of Simpson's paradox: a strong local signal is destroyed by inappropriate aggregation. To correct for this, we introduce a learned local calibration step grounded in Bayesian decision theory. Rather than aggregating raw token scores, we first learn lightweight predictors of the score distributions conditioned on position in hidden space, and aggregate calibrated log-likelihood ratios instead. This single intervention dramatically and consistently improves detection performance across all baseline detectors and all datasets we consider. For example, our calibrated variant of Fast-DetectGPT improves AUROC from $0.63$ to $0.85$ on GPT-5.4 text, and a locally-calibrated DMAP detector we introduce achieves state-of-the-art performance across the board. That said, our central contribution is not a new detector, but a precise diagnosis of a significant cause of under-performance of existing detectors and a principled, modular remedy compatible with any token-averaging pipeline. This will serve as a foundation for the community to build upon, with natural avenues including richer distributional models, improved calibration strategies, and principled ensembling with hidden-space geometry signals via the full Bayes-optimal decision rule.
2605.06294.paper_summary_zh: 人類撰寫的文本與大型語言模型生成的文本之間可靠區分的能力對社會具有深遠的重要性。這個問題的主導方法利用了可能性假設：機器生成的文本對檢測語言模型來說應該顯得比人類撰寫的文本更為可能。然而，我們證明了在檢測模型的隱藏空間中，區分人類和機器文本的標記級信號是不均勻的，而大多數檢測器所做的天真平均基於可能性的標記分數，會導致一種辛普森悖論：強大的局部信號因不當聚合而被破壞。為了解決這個問題，我們引入了一個基於貝葉斯決策理論的學習本地校準步驟。我們首先學習基於隱藏空間位置的分數分佈的輕量預測器，而不是直接聚合原始標記分數，然後聚合校準的對數可能性比率。這一單一干預措施在我們考慮的所有基準檢測器和所有數據集上顯著且持續地改善了檢測性能。例如，我們的校準變體 Fast-DetectGPT 在 GPT-5.4 文本上將 AUROC 從 $0.63$ 提升至 $0.85$，而我們引入的本地校準 DMAP 檢測器則在各方面達到了最先進的性能。話雖如此，我們的核心貢獻不是一個新的檢測器，而是對現有檢測器性能不足的一個重要原因的精確診斷，以及一個與任何標記平均管道兼容的原則性模組化解決方案。這將為社群提供一個基礎，以便進一步發展，自然的途徑包括更豐富的分佈模型、改進的校準策略，以及通過完整的貝葉斯最優決策規則與隱藏空間幾何信號的原則性集成。
2605.06294.author: Tom Kempton et.al.
2605.06294.authors: Tom Kempton, Viktor Drobnyi, Maeve Madigan, Stuart Burrell
